card.title: The Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)
card.teaser: Why 80% of your results come from just 20% of your effort — and how an Italian economist spotted it in pea pods.
card.coverImagePrompt: An elegant pie chart rendered as stained glass, one dominant slice glowing warm gold while the rest fades into muted blues. Conceptual, painterly, cinematic lighting.

lessons.title: A gardener's strange observation
lessons.body: In 1896, Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto was pacing his garden when he noticed something odd: roughly 20% of his pea pods were producing 80% of the peas. Curious, he checked Italy's tax records — and found that 20% of the population owned 80% of the land. The same ratio kept showing up everywhere he looked. He had stumbled onto one of the most durable patterns in nature and economics.

visual.imagePrompt: Close-up of an Italian garden with pea vines, some pods plump and overflowing, others sparse, golden afternoon light

lessons.title: The power law in plain English
lessons.body: The Pareto Principle says that in many systems, a small number of inputs cause the majority of outputs. Twenty percent of customers generate 80% of revenue. Twenty percent of bugs cause 80% of crashes. It's not a law of nature — it's a pattern produced by 'power-law distributions', where outcomes are wildly unequal rather than clustered around an average.

keyTerms.definition: A distribution where a few items dominate results, unlike a bell curve
A rough heuristic that roughly 80% of outcomes come from 20% of causes

visual.imagePrompt: A long-tail bar chart with one massive bar on the left and many tiny ones trailing off to the right

lessons.title: It keeps showing up
lessons.body: The pattern is shockingly robust. Microsoft found that fixing the top 20% of reported bugs eliminated 80% of errors. Retailers regularly see 80% of revenue come from 20% of SKUs. Even Wikipedia — written by volunteers — was shaped by a tiny fraction of contributors writing the overwhelming majority of edits. The exact numbers vary, but the asymmetry is real.
lessons.funFact: The principle wasn't named 'Pareto' until 1941, when quality engineer Joseph Juran applied it to manufacturing defects and gave it a catchy label.

visual.imagePrompt: A magnifying glass hovering over a spreadsheet, highlighting a handful of rows glowing orange while the rest stay grey

lessons.title: Using it without obsessing over it
lessons.body: The real value of 80/20 isn't the exact ratio — it's the habit of asking, 'Which small slice of what I'm doing is producing most of the results?' Identify that slice. Protect it. Then honestly consider whether the other 80% of your effort is worth keeping at all. Most people who try this for a week find at least one task they can quietly drop forever.

quiz.question: What did Vilfredo Pareto originally observe?
options: That markets always return to equilibrium
That 20% of Italy's population owned 80% of the land
That inflation follows a yearly cycle
quiz.explanation: Pareto noticed the same 80/20 ratio in his pea pods and in Italian land ownership.

quiz.question: The 80/20 rule is an example of what kind of distribution?
quiz.explanation: Power-law distributions produce extreme inequality in outcomes — exactly what 80/20 describes.

quiz.question: Who coined the name 'Pareto Principle'?
quiz.explanation: Quality engineer Joseph Juran applied the pattern to defects in 1941 and gave it its modern name.

sources.title: The 80/20 Principle: The Secret to Achieving More with Less
